title: GM Complications
description: Wrinkles and friction to inject when a scene needs a complication — triggered by mixed results, failed rolls, or stalled momentum.

entries.result: A third party arrives at the worst possible moment and has opinions about what they see.
Something the party was relying on turns out to be broken, spent, or no longer available.
The target has already moved. Whoever or whatever was here is gone.
A bystander gets involved — not by choice, and not helpfully.
Something makes a sound. It carries further than expected.
A piece of information the party had turns out to be wrong. Not outdated — wrong.
The person the party needs is unavailable — and whoever is covering for them is less cooperative.
Something that was hidden is now visible. This cuts both ways.
A clock the party didn't know existed suddenly becomes relevant. Time is shorter than assumed.
The situation has a witness the party hadn't accounted for.
A deal or agreement the party made earlier is called in at the worst time.
The environment itself becomes a problem — weather, terrain, or structural failure.
Something the party did earlier has caught up with them. It wasn't forgotten.
A resource runs out earlier than expected. The margin was smaller than assumed.
Two of the party's goals have become mutually exclusive. A choice is required.
The opposition is better prepared than anticipated. Someone gave them information.
A member of the party is recognized — and not fondly.
The prize turns out to be guarded, claimed, or already compromised.
Help arrives — but with its own agenda that may not align with the party's.
The easy path is watched. The hard path is not. Both are still options.
Something the party thought was an asset turns out to be a liability in this context.
A rumor about the party has preceded them. It's not entirely accurate, but it's not nothing.
The local rules here are different from what the party assumed. Someone is about to find out.
A plan is working — on the wrong target.
The situation has a hidden layer. What's visible is not what's actually happening.
A neutral party takes sides. It is not the side the party would have chosen.
Someone loses something important. It wasn't secured as well as assumed.
The opposition makes a move the party didn't anticipate and couldn't have predicted.
A piece of the plan that required precision hits friction and slows everything down.
An escape route is blocked. A different one exists, but it comes with a cost.
Someone tells the truth when a lie was expected, and it complicates everything.
A previous success turns out to have been too visible. People are connecting dots.
A tool or item works differently in this context than it did before.
The goal is achieved, but the method creates a new problem that wasn't anticipated.
Something that was in the party's favor reverses without warning.
A delay occurs — mundane, frustrating, and poorly timed.
The party is split in a moment when being split is a problem.
Someone outside the party makes a decision that directly affects the party's plan.
The layout of the space is not what was described. Something is in the wrong place.
A piece of information the party needs is available — from the worst possible source.
Something small goes wrong, and fixing it draws attention that wasn't wanted.
The opposition has leverage that the party didn't know existed.
A friend of the party has been put in a difficult position — not by the party, but because of them.
The situation changes faster than the plan can adapt.
A secondary objective comes into conflict with the primary one. Something has to give.
A location the party assumed was safe has been compromised.
The opposition is not who the party thought. This changes the calculus.
A consequence from a past session resurfaces at the wrong moment.
Something breaks. The repair is doable, but the timing is awful.
The party is being watched, and they can't be sure for how long.
An NPC the party trusted is acting strangely. It may be nothing. It may not be.
The mission is on track, but someone in the party is not.
A gate, lock, or barrier that wasn't expected is now blocking progress.
Something the party was carrying draws the wrong kind of attention.
The party achieves the goal — and immediately realizes there's a step they missed.
A competitor arrives with the same goal and no interest in sharing.
The contact the party was counting on has problems of their own right now.
The party is being followed. Whether this is a threat depends on who is following them.
A piece of the plan requires something the party doesn't currently have.
The timeline has compressed. What was days is now hours.
A well-meaning NPC makes things worse by trying to help.
Something in the environment was recently disturbed. The party is not the first here.
An old enemy isn't the problem — but they're in the area, and that's a distraction.
The plan relies on an assumption that hasn't been verified. It should have been.
Someone asks a direct question the party would rather not answer honestly.
A shortcut introduces a variable that the longer route wouldn't have.
The situation is being escalated by a party the group hasn't dealt with before.
A noise, a smell, a flash of light — something has been noticed and the cause is unclear.
The party's reputation works against them here in a way they couldn't have anticipated.
Someone in the area is having a crisis that has nothing to do with the party — but it's in the way.
A piece of the environment is unstable. It wasn't obvious until someone got close.
The opposition has already prepared for the most obvious approach. A different one is needed.
Someone who was supposed to stay out of it has gotten involved.
A distraction works — too well. Something important is now unattended.
The party gains access to what they need, but so does someone else.
A promise made informally is being treated as binding.
Something that looked like cover turns out not to be.
An NPC's loyalty is tested by the situation, and the result is uncertain.
The route back is no longer clear. The way out requires navigating something new.
A piece of equipment fails at a critical moment. It's repairable — later.
The objective has been moved, swapped, or replaced. What's here is not what was promised.
A witness to something the party would rather not be witnessed is more alert than expected.
The party's approach has a flaw that only becomes visible when it's too late to fully correct.
Something that was an advantage at the start of the scene is now neutral or worse.
A door that was unlocked is now locked. Someone has been here recently.
An unexpected cost is attached to the path of least resistance.
The person the party was trying to avoid is exactly where they didn't want them to be.
A piece of the plan required secrecy. Secrecy has been partially compromised.
The party is asked to make a decision before they have the information they need to make it well.
Something the party intended to leave behind has been picked up by someone else.
A distraction the party created draws more attention than the thing they were distracting from.
An authority figure enters the situation and wants to know what's going on.
One member of the party has been compromised or cornered while separated.
The party's timing was off. They arrived at the right place at the wrong moment.
A detail in the plan that seemed minor turns out to be load-bearing.
The opposition isn't hostile — they're confused — and confused people make unpredictable decisions.
An innocent is caught in the middle and will be affected by whatever happens next.
The party is making progress — but so is a competing faction working toward something adjacent.
Something has been tampered with. Whether it was the opposition or someone else is unclear.
An NPC who should be an obstacle turns out to have common cause with the party — but at a cost.
The environment changes — light shifts, weather turns, a crowd gathers, a space empties.
A piece of information the party is acting on is a few hours out of date.
The opposition makes a mistake that helps the party — but in a way that raises new questions.
Someone in the party is recognized from something unrelated. It's awkward.
A resource the party needed to preserve has been partially used without anyone noticing.
The party's cover story has a gap that someone just noticed.
An item or piece of knowledge the party has is suddenly more valuable to someone else than expected.
The opposition has backup that wasn't accounted for in the original assessment.
A trap that wasn't meant for the party catches them anyway.
The local population reacts to the party's presence in an unexpected way.
A shortcut was taken. The thing skipped over turns out to matter.
The party's exit is narrowing. The window to leave cleanly is getting smaller.
A small error compounds into a larger one before it can be corrected.
Something the party did for one reason has been interpreted by others as something else.
An NPC who was neutral has become motivated by recent events. Their motivation is unclear.
The party has been given a false lead — not maliciously, just from someone who didn't know better.
A piece of the environment interacts badly with something the party is carrying.
Two members of the party have conflicting ideas about the right move, and both have valid points.
The scene's most obvious exit leads somewhere worse than staying.
Someone who was supposed to be an obstacle is having a terrible day and wants to talk about it.
The party's previous solution to this kind of problem doesn't apply here.
A helpful NPC has given information they believed to be accurate. It is not.
Something the party created or set in motion earlier is now working against them.
A guard, lock, or barrier appears that isn't on any map or description the party has.
The party finds what they came for — and finds evidence that someone knows they were coming.
A change in the social landscape — a new alliance, a grudge, a shift in status — has altered the terrain.
The information the party gathered was correct when gathered. It has since changed.
Someone is using the party as cover for their own agenda, and it's starting to show.
The opposition is more sympathetic than expected. This doesn't change the situation, but it complicates it.
A party member's personal history with someone in the scene becomes relevant and unhelpful.
The scene's most powerful player has just changed sides, or appears to have.
The party gets what they came for. Unfortunately, it is also bait.
A single point of failure in the plan has just been discovered — and activated.
The party finds evidence that the true scope of the problem is larger than they understood.
Something that seemed minor in an earlier scene is now a major obstacle.
A trusted NPC cannot be found when needed. Their absence is unexplained.
An unintended consequence of the party's last action is now visible and demands attention.
The party's allies have made a move without coordinating, and it complicates this moment.
The party is offered a genuine choice between two things they both want. They can only take one.
A piece of leverage the party had is called in by the other side before the party can use it.
Something is wrong with the information the party is relying on — and the flaw isn't obvious.
A faction the party has been ignoring has decided the party is relevant to their interests.
The party's timeline is now shared with a stranger whose schedule isn't compatible.
An ally acts on good intentions and makes the situation measurably worse.
The opposition gets lucky. Sometimes that happens.
A long-running deception comes undone. Not catastrophically — but it needs managing.
Something the party assumed would take minutes takes hours. The delay has consequences.
An NPC asks for something small before cooperating. The ask is inconvenient.
The goal has been partially achieved. The remaining piece is the hard piece.
The easy option has an obvious downside. The hard option has a hidden one.
Something the party doesn't recognize turns out to be a significant threat.
A decision made by a party member without consultation has downstream consequences.
An NPC's loyalty is not in question — but their judgment is.
The party's reputation precedes them in a way that makes the current goal harder.
Two things the party needs are in locations that cannot both be reached in the available time.
Something was stored here and has since been moved. Someone is going to have to find it.
A party member's action, however reasonable, has been misread by a key NPC.
The most direct route is no longer usable. A workaround exists but adds time.
A hidden interested party makes their presence known. Their angle is not immediately clear.
The party is asked to vouch for someone or something. The ask is not unreasonable. The timing is.
Something that should have been confirmed was assumed. It was wrong to assume it.
A useful NPC has been neutralized — not by the opposition, but by circumstance.
The party's plan works, but the success is visible to more people than intended.
A small secret comes out. It isn't damning. But it changes how someone sees the party.
An unexpected cost is extracted at the moment of success. Refusing it undoes the win.
The party finds something they weren't looking for. It raises more questions than it answers.
A peripheral character the party has overlooked turns out to hold a key piece of the puzzle.
A favor the party did for someone else has created an expectation they didn't intend to create.
The opposition's next move is visible — but stopping it requires abandoning the current goal.
The party has to work with someone they don't trust. The task genuinely requires them.
Something in the plan was delegated. The person it was delegated to didn't do it.
An external event — a public announcement, a brawl, a fire — disrupts the local environment.
A piece of the scene that seemed decorative turns out to be functional. And fragile.
The party's next step requires someone they burned bridges with.
Everything is going according to plan. That hasn't happened in a while. Something is wrong.
The goal is achieved, but a secondary cost — social, material, or ethical — is attached.
A rumor has circulated that the party is responsible for something they didn't do. It's not entirely implausible.
The party is being played against another group. Both groups have legitimate grievances.
A map, plan, or diagram the party has been using is subtly inaccurate.
An NPC who has been helpful up to now has reached the limit of what they're willing to risk.
The party achieves their objective — and realizes they've also fulfilled someone else's.
A piece of equipment is working but giving unreliable readings or output.
Something the party dismissed as irrelevant turns out to be central.
An ally's recent success has made them overconfident, and they're about to act on it.
The party is right about the situation — but cannot currently prove it to anyone who matters.
A faction's internal dispute spills into the space the party is operating in.
An escape plan requires a distraction. The distraction has consequences.
Something the party has been protecting is at risk from a direction they weren't watching.
The scene ends earlier than expected. What the party didn't finish stays unfinished.
Someone the party thought was uninvolved has been watching the whole time and takes action.
A plan designed for five people has to be executed by three. Something has to be cut.
A character connection — personal history, a debt, a shared loss — surfaces at an inconvenient moment.
The party has been operating on the assumption that someone doesn't know something. They do.
A piece of information the party got from an enemy turns out to be accurate. The implications are uncomfortable.
The plan requires a step that is technically possible and morally complicated.
Something that should have prevented this problem was bypassed by someone else before the party arrived.
A failure state the party planned for occurs — just not in the scenario they planned for it.
The scene resolves. Cleanly, even. Something about how clean it was feels wrong.
The party gets what they needed — and in doing so, tips someone off that they needed it.
Everything ends where it was supposed to. Only the party knows how close it came to not.
